title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Three easy animation ideas




Level 1


Fade-in entrance
Content appears softly when the slide step is revealed.

opacity + translateY + keyframes






Level 2


Hover lift
A card slightly rises and gains shadow when the mouse moves over it.

transition + transform + box-shadow






Level 3


Button micro-interaction
Small motion on buttons makes your page feel more interactive.

Click me






These three are a good starting point because they are easy to build, easy to control, and already make a website look much more polished.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
How these animations actually work

The basic logic


1. Add a class to the element
In HTML, a class is just a label you attach to an element so CSS can style it.
HTML: <div class="hover-lift">This card lifts on hover</div>
Quarto: ::: {.hover-lift} This card lifts on hover :::
In other words, Quarto’s ::: block often plays the same role as an HTML <div>.


2. Define the style in CSS
Then CSS tells that class what to do. A simplified example：
.hover-lift { transition: transform 0.3s ease; }
.hover-lift:hover { transform: translateY(-8px); }


3. Trigger the animation
For entrance effects, RevealJS can reveal a fragment step by step. For hover effects, the trigger is simply :hover.

Inspiration

Design inspiration
Dribbble → animation + UI ideas

Tools & Code

Animation resources
CodePen → ready-to-use CSS demos

UI libraries (Tailwind UI, Flowbite)

Copy → tweak → integrate into Quarto





Good animation design is rarely invented from scratch. Most of the time, you observe → copy → simplify → adapt.


Final tip

Think like a designer, not just a coder
Don’t add animation everywhere, use it in an appropriate way

text:
Why animation matters


Without animation

feels static
less visual interest
looks like plain notes


With animation

draws attention
feels modern
more polished



Even very small motion can make a Quarto site feel more intentional and more professional.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Three easy animation ideas




Level 1

Fade-in entrance
Content appears softly when the slide step is revealed.

opacity + translateY + keyframes





Level 2

Hover lift
A card slightly rises and gains shadow when the mouse moves over it.

transition + transform + box-shadow





Level 3

Button micro-interaction
Small motion on buttons makes your page feel more interactive.

Click me




These three are a good starting point because they are easy to build, easy to control, and already make a website look much more polished.

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
How these animations actually work
The basic logic

1. Add a class to the element
In HTML, a class is just a label you attach to an element so CSS can style it.
HTML: <div class="hover-lift">This card lifts on hover</div>
Quarto: ::: {.hover-lift} This card lifts on hover :::
In other words, Quarto’s ::: block often plays the same role as an HTML <div>.


2. Define the style in CSS
Then CSS tells that class what to do. A simplified example:
.hover-lift { transition: transform 0.3s ease; }
.hover-lift:hover { transform: translateY(-8px); }


3. Trigger the animation
For entrance effects, RevealJS can reveal a fragment step by step. For hover effects, the trigger is simply :hover.

Fancy 03

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Takeaway

Start with one entrance animation such as fade-in
Add one interaction pattern such as hover lift
Improve buttons with micro-interactions
Use fancier effects only where you want emphasis

title: How to Add Simple Animations to Your Quarto Website
text:
Where to find inspiration
If you want to use animation to make a website look better, there are many good places to find ideas and examples.



Resource 01

CodePen
A huge collection of live animation demos. You can search for ideas like CSS animation, hover effects, cards, buttons, or page transitions.



Resource 02

Awwwards
A gallery of high-end website design. This is useful for design inspiration and for seeing how motion is used in real websites.



Resource 03

Dribbble
A good place to find modern UI ideas, micro-interactions, and polished visual styles.



Resource 04

GitHub and public Quarto examples
You can look at public Quarto websites and templates to borrow layout ideas, animation ideas, and CSS structure.



Resource 05

ChatGPT or other AI tools
AI can help generate CSS and animation ideas quickly, but it is still important to read the code and understand it.



Resource 06

CSS-Tricks
A useful site for learning how CSS animations and transitions actually work.



A good workflow is: find inspiration, simplify the idea, and adapt it to your own website style. You do not need very complex effects — even small motion can make a page feel more polished.

title: Coursework
text:
I want this page to do more than list course names. My goal is to show how I am learning to move from business intuition to empirical reasoning.
Through graduate study, I want to become better at:

framing messy business questions as researchable analytical problems
distinguishing correlation from causal interpretation
explaining quantitative evidence in plain language
connecting statistical tools to real decision settings rather than treating them as isolated techniques

title: Coursework
text:
Relevant Coursework


A/B Testing (MGTA 495)


Simulation, inference, and experimental reasoning in digital product settings.


This post uses a newsletter sign-up experiment to explain how a business question becomes a statistical one. It walks through treatment effects, bootstrap standard errors, the central limit theorem, large-sample testing, and the regression interpretation of an A/B test.


Key Ideas



experimental design


bootstrap standard errors


central limit theorem


large-sample hypothesis testing


regression interpretation of treatment effects





Causal Identification (MGTA 495)


A blog-style walkthrough of Card & Krueger’s minimum wage study.


This post revisits one of the most famous natural experiments in empirical economics: New Jersey’s 1992 minimum wage increase. Rather than treating the paper as a set of tables to copy, the post focuses on the logic behind the design — why Pennsylvania works as a comparison group, what the difference-in-differences estimate is actually removing, and why the regression table matters as a stress test rather than a formality.


Key Ideas



difference-in-differences


treatment and control group logic


treatment intensity


robustness across model specifications


causal interpretation in observational data

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

You have a website and your landing page includes a place where visitors can sign up to receive your newsletter.
You want to test which of two possible “calls to action” (CTAs) leads to more sign-ups. CTA A reads “Sign up for our newsletter here!” and CTA B reads “Stay up to date by signing up!” (you are welcome to change the language of the CTAs).
You will evaluate which CTA performs better by running an A/B test. Explain what an A/B test is: visitors are randomly assigned to see one of the two CTAs, and you compare sign-up rates between the two groups.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem






Note



In this section, connect the A/B test to a formal statistical framework. Address each of the following ideas:

Each visitor either signs up or does not, so we can model each visitor’s outcome as a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\), the probability of signing up.
Because the CTA a visitor sees may influence their probability of signing up, we allow the parameter to differ across groups: visitors who see CTA A have sign-up probability \(\pi_A\), and visitors who see CTA B have sign-up probability \(\pi_B\). Each visitor can either sign up (1) or not (0).
The quantity we care about is \(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\), the difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.
Our estimator of \(\theta\) is the difference in sample means, \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\). (Since each observation is either 0 or 1, the sample mean is simply the proportion of visitors who signed up — so \(\bar{X}_A = \hat\pi_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat\pi_B\).)

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data






Note




Write a brief paragraph for the reader explaining that in the real world we would not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\), but for the purpose of this exercise we will set them ourselves so that we can study the behavior of our estimator when we know the truth.
Suppose \(\pi_A = 0.22\) and \(\pi_B = 0.18\), so the true difference is \(\theta = 0.04\).
Using a language of your choice, simulate 1,000 draws from each of the two Bernoulli distributions (2,000 total). Store the results so you can use them in the sections that follow.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers






Note




Write an introductory sentence or two explaining the Law of Large Numbers to the reader: as the sample size grows, the sample mean converges to the population mean. Connect this back to our estimator — the LLN is what gives us confidence that \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\) will be close to the true \(\theta\) when our sample is large enough.
Then demonstrate this visually. Using the simulated data from the previous section, compute the element-wise differences between the CTA A draws and the CTA B draws to get a vector of 1,000 differences. Calculate the cumulative average of that vector (i.e., the running mean after 1 draw, after 2 draws, …, after 1,000 draws) and plot it against the number of observations.
The plot should show a noisy average when only a few observations are included that settles down and converges toward the true difference of 0.04 as the sample size grows.
After the plot, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should take away from it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors






Note




Now that we “trust” our estimator, the next natural question is: how precise is it? We would like to report not just a point estimate of \(\theta\) but also a confidence interval that communicates our uncertainty.
Write a short introduction explaining the idea of bootstrapping to the reader. The bootstrap is a resampling method that lets us estimate the variability of a statistic without relying on a closed-form formula. We resample (with replacement) from our observed data many times, compute the statistic of interest on each resample, and use the standard deviation of those resampled statistics as our estimate of the standard error.
Then carry out the following steps:

From your 1,000 CTA A draws and 1,000 CTA B draws, draw a bootstrap resample of size 1,000 (with replacement) from each group. Compute \(\hat\theta^* = \bar{X}_A^* - \bar{X}_B^*\) for that resample.
Repeat step 1 a total of 1,000 times to produce a vector of 1,000 bootstrapped estimates of \(\theta\).
Compute the standard deviation of those 1,000 values. This is your bootstrapped standard error.
Compare it to the analytical standard error. For the difference in means of two independent Bernoulli samples, the standard error formula is:


\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
where \(\hat\pi_A\) and \(\hat\pi_B\) are the sample proportions and \(n_A\) and \(n_B\) are the sample sizes.

Report both the bootstrapped standard error and the analytical standard error and comment on how close they are to each other. Then use the bootstrapped standard error to construct a 95% confidence interval for \(\theta\) and interpret it for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem






Note




Write a short introduction explaining the Central Limit Theorem to the reader: regardless of the shape of the underlying population distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean (and therefore of the difference in sample means) becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows.
Then demonstrate this visually by creating four histograms, one for each of the following sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).
For each sample size \(n\):

Draw \(n\) observations from the CTA A distribution and \(n\) observations from the CTA B distribution.
Compute \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).
Repeat steps 1–2 a total of 1,000 times to produce 1,000 values of \(\hat\theta\).
Plot a histogram of those 1,000 values.

Arrange the four histograms so they can be compared easily (e.g., in a 2x2 grid with consistent x-axis scales).
After the plots, write a few sentences explaining what the reader should observe: at small sample sizes the histogram may look rough or asymmetric, but as \(n\) increases the distribution of \(\hat\theta\) becomes smoother and more bell-shaped, illustrating the Central Limit Theorem in action.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing






Note




Now that we have seen the CLT in action — that the sampling distribution of \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples — we can use this result to perform a formal hypothesis test.
Write a paragraph explaining what a hypothesis test is to the reader. We set up a null hypothesis \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (i.e., the two CTAs produce the same sign-up rate) and an alternative hypothesis \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\). The goal is to assess whether the data provide enough evidence to reject \(H_0\).
Then explain why we standardize \(\hat\theta\) to form a test statistic. We compute:

\[
z = \frac{\hat\theta - 0}{SE(\hat\theta)}
\]

Walk the reader through the logic of this standardization step by step:

By the CLT, we know that \(\hat\theta\) is approximately Normal for large samples. Under \(H_0\), the mean of that distribution is 0.
When we divide a Normal random variable by its standard deviation, we get a standard Normal \(N(0,1)\). So under \(H_0\), \(z \;\dot\sim\; N(0,1)\).
This means we know the distribution of our test statistic under the null, which is exactly what we need to compute a p-value.

Explain to the reader why this is sometimes called a “t-test” even though the test statistic follows an approximate Normal distribution, not a t-distribution. The classical t-test arises when the data are drawn from a Normal distribution and the variance must be estimated — the ratio then follows an exact t-distribution. In our setting the data are Bernoulli (not Normal), so there is no exact t-distribution result. The CLT gives us approximate Normality, and since we estimate the SE from the data, what we are really doing is closer to a z-test. In practice, for large samples the t-distribution and the standard Normal are nearly identical, so the distinction has little practical consequence — but it is important to understand why.
Using your simulated data, compute the test statistic and its associated p-value (using the standard Normal distribution as the reference). Interpret the result for the reader.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression






Note




It turns out that the two-sample test we just ran is mathematically equivalent to a simple linear regression. Understanding this connection is useful because it means the same machinery — and the same software — that you use for regression can also perform hypothesis tests, and it generalizes naturally to more complex experimental designs.
Explain the setup to the reader. Stack the CTA A and CTA B data into a single dataset with two columns: an outcome \(Y_i\) (1 if the visitor signed up, 0 otherwise) and an indicator \(D_i\) (1 if the visitor saw CTA A, 0 if they saw CTA B). Then fit the regression:

\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]

Walk through the interpretation of the coefficients:

\(\beta_0\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 0\) — i.e., the mean of the B group, which estimates \(\pi_B\).
\(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the expected value of \(Y_i\) when \(D_i = 1\) — i.e., the mean of the A group, which estimates \(\pi_A\).
Therefore \(\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta\), and the OLS estimate \(\hat\beta_1\) is numerically identical to \(\hat\theta = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\).

Fit this regression using your simulated data. Report the coefficient estimate \(\hat\beta_1\), its standard error, its t-statistic, and its p-value. Compare these to the values you computed in the previous section using the two-sample approach — they should match (up to a small difference in how the standard error is computed; standard OLS assumes a single pooled variance, while the two-sample formula we used assumes separate variances for each group).
Explain to the reader why this equivalence matters: the regression framework makes it easy to add controls, interactions, or additional treatment arms, all while producing the same inference in the simple case.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking






Note




Your boss is impatient. Rather than waiting until all 1,000 visitors have been assigned to each group, she wants to run the hypothesis test after every 100 visitors per group — peeking at the results after 100, 200, 300, …, up to 1,000 visitors per group (10 tests in total). If any of those tests comes back significant at the \(\alpha = 0.05\) level, she wants to declare a winner and stop the experiment early.
Explain to the reader why this is problematic under classical frequentist statistics. The key issue is that a single test at \(\alpha = 0.05\) has a 5% chance of a false positive (rejecting \(H_0\) when it is actually true). But when you conduct multiple tests on accumulating data, each peek is another opportunity to get a false positive. The overall probability of at least one false rejection across all the peeks is much higher than 5%.
Demonstrate this with a simulation. The idea is as follows:

Set up a world where the null hypothesis is true: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\) (i.e., \(\theta = 0\), there is no difference between the CTAs).
Simulate a single experiment: draw 1,000 observations for group A and 1,000 for group B. After every 100 observations per group (at \(n = 100, 200, \ldots, 1000\)), compute the z-statistic and p-value. Record whether any of the 10 p-values falls below 0.05.
Repeat step 2 a total of 10,000 times to simulate 10,000 experiments.
Compute the fraction of those 10,000 experiments in which at least one peek produced a significant result. This is the empirical false positive rate under peeking.

Compare this to the nominal 5% false positive rate of a single test. Report the result and explain to the reader why peeking inflates the Type I error rate and what this means in practice for running A/B tests.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
A landing page usually asks for only a tiny action from a visitor: one click, one email address, one moment of attention. Yet small wording changes can make that request feel either inviting or easy to ignore. That is exactly why call-to-action copy is such a natural setting for A/B testing.
Imagine a homepage with a newsletter signup box. One version of the page uses the line CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!” A second version uses CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!” The business question is simple: which message leads to more subscriptions?
An A/B test answers that question by splitting incoming traffic at random. Some visitors see version A and others see version B. Once enough people have passed through the page, the two sign-up rates can be compared. Behind that very practical workflow sits a deeper statistical story about estimation, uncertainty, sampling variation, and false positives. The goal of this post is to use one simple experiment to walk through those core ideas in classical frequentist inference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
A landing page usually asks for only a tiny action from a visitor: one click, one email address, one moment of attention. Yet small wording changes can make that request feel either inviting or easy to ignore. That is exactly why call-to-action copy is such a natural setting for A/B testing.
Imagine a homepage with a newsletter signup box. One version of the page uses the line CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!” A second version uses CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!” The business question is simple: which message leads to more subscriptions?
An A/B test answers that question by splitting incoming traffic at random. Some visitors see version A and others see version B. Once enough people have passed through the page, the two sign-up rates can be compared. Behind that very practical workflow sits a deeper statistical story about estimation, uncertainty, sampling variation, and false positives. The goal of this post is to use one simple experiment to walk through those core ideas in classical frequentist inference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
At the individual level, each visitor generates a binary outcome: they either subscribe or they do not. That makes the Bernoulli model a natural starting point. If \(\pi\) denotes the probability that a visitor signs up, then each observation is a draw from a Bernoulli distribution with parameter \(\pi\).
The key complication is that the probability of signing up may depend on which CTA the visitor sees. Let \(\pi_A\) denote the sign-up probability under CTA A and let \(\pi_B\) denote the sign-up probability under CTA B. The quantity of real interest is not either probability by itself, but their difference:
\[
\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B
\]
If \(\theta\) is positive, CTA A performs better. If it is negative, CTA B performs better. If it is zero, the two messages are equally effective.
In practice, the true value of \(\theta\) is unknown and must be estimated from data. A natural estimator is the difference in sample means:
\[
\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B
\]
Because each outcome is either 0 or 1, the sample mean in each group is just the observed sign-up rate. So \(\bar{X}_A = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat{\pi}_B\). In other words, the estimator here is nothing more exotic than the observed difference in conversion rates across the two page variants.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real experiment, the truth sits behind the data and is never directly visible. Simulation turns that relationship around: the truth is specified first, and the observed data are generated from it. That makes simulation a useful way to study how a statistical method behaves when the answer is already known.
For the rest of this post, suppose CTA A has a true sign-up probability of \(0.22\) and CTA B has a true sign-up probability of \(0.18\). Under those assumptions, the true treatment effect is
\[
\theta = 0.22 - 0.18 = 0.04
\]
So the “better” CTA improves sign-up rates by four percentage points. The analysis below begins by simulating 1,000 visitors for each group, creating a synthetic experiment with 2,000 total observations.








Simulated A/B Test Data


1,000 visitors per CTA


Group

Simulated sample proportion

CTA A
22.0%

CTA B
18.0%
17.2%

As expected, the simulated sample proportions are close to the truth but not identical to it. That small gap is not a mistake; it is the whole point. Even when the underlying probabilities are fixed, random samples still wobble.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
One reason analysts trust sample averages at all is the Law of Large Numbers. Informally, it says that averages become more stable as more observations accumulate. In the A/B setting, that idea explains why the difference in observed sign-up rates eventually becomes a reliable guide to the true treatment effect.
To make that visible, the two simulated outcome vectors can be differenced observation by observation, and the running average of those differences can be tracked as the sample grows from 1 pair to 1,000 pairs.









The beginning of the curve is chaotic. With only a handful of observations, one extra signup or one extra non-signup can move the average sharply. But that instability fades as the sample grows. The curve does not become perfectly flat, yet it clearly settles toward the true effect. This is the first major lesson of the post: large samples do not eliminate randomness, but they do discipline it.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate answers only one question: what effect seems most plausible given the observed data? It does not answer the equally important question of how noisy that estimate is. Two experiments may produce the same estimated treatment effect but differ dramatically in how uncertain that estimate should feel.
The bootstrap offers a practical way to measure that uncertainty. Rather than relying entirely on a closed-form formula, the bootstrap repeatedly resamples from the observed data, computes the statistic of interest in each resample, and then uses the spread of those resampled statistics as an estimate of the standard error.








Bootstrap Results


Uncertainty around the treatment effect


Statistic
Value




Estimated treatment effect
3.90%


Bootstrap standard error
1.71%


Analytical standard error
1.76%


95% CI lower bound
0.55%


95% CI upper bound
7.25%
















Two things stand out here. First, the bootstrap standard error and the analytical standard error are very close, which is a reassuring sign that the uncertainty estimate is not an artifact of one particular method. Second, the resulting 95% confidence interval excludes zero. In plain language, the observed data are not just consistent with CTA A being better; they are inconsistent with the idea that the two messages perform equally well.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The next step is to move from one experiment to the behavior of the estimator across many hypothetical repetitions of the experiment. This is where the Central Limit Theorem becomes useful. Even when the underlying data are Bernoulli rather than Normally distributed, the sampling distribution of a sample mean becomes approximately Normal as the sample size grows. Because \(\hat{\theta}\) is built from sample means, the same logic applies to it as well.
To show that visually, the simulation below repeats the entire A/B test 1,000 times for each of four sample sizes: \(n \in \{25, 50, 100, 500\}\).









The smaller-sample histograms are rough and uneven, which makes sense: with limited data, there is still a lot of discreteness and noise in the estimator. But as the sample size increases, the histograms become smoother, tighter, and more bell-shaped. That is the CLT at work. It is also the bridge from descriptive simulation to formal inference, because approximate Normality is what makes z-based testing possible in large samples.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
At this point the analysis has everything needed for a formal test. The business question becomes a statistical one: could an observed difference of this size reasonably have appeared if the two CTAs were actually identical?
The null and alternative hypotheses are
\[
H_0: \theta = 0
\]
and
\[
H_1: \theta \neq 0
\]
respectively.
The observed estimate is converted into a test statistic by scaling it relative to its own uncertainty:
\[
z = \frac{\hat{\theta} - 0}{SE(\hat{\theta})}
\]
That standardization matters. Under the CLT, \(\hat{\theta}\) is approximately Normal in large samples, and under the null its mean is zero. Once divided by its standard error, the statistic is approximately distributed as \(N(0,1)\). That reference distribution is what makes a p-value possible.
Strictly speaking, this setup is closer to a large-sample z-test than to a classical t-test. The exact t framework belongs to Normal data with estimated variance. Here the data are Bernoulli, so the Normal approximation comes from asymptotics rather than an exact finite-sample theorem. In a sample this large, the distinction is practically minor, but conceptually it is worth keeping straight.








Hypothesis Test for the Difference in Sign-up Rates


Large-sample z-based inference

The p-value is small enough to reject the null at conventional significance levels. In practical terms, the observed gap between the two CTAs is too large to dismiss as ordinary sampling noise. The data point toward a real performance difference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
One of the most elegant parts of this example is that the two-sample test can be rewritten as a regression with almost no extra machinery. That matters because regression is the language most empirical work eventually speaks.
Stack the two groups into one dataset. Let \(Y_i\) equal 1 if visitor \(i\) signs up and 0 otherwise, and let \(D_i\) equal 1 if that visitor saw CTA A and 0 if they saw CTA B. Then estimate
\[
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 D_i + \varepsilon_i
\]
In this model, \(\beta_0\) is the mean outcome for the B group, so it estimates \(\pi_B\). The sum \(\beta_0 + \beta_1\) is the mean outcome for the A group, so it estimates \(\pi_A\). That means \(\beta_1\) itself is exactly the treatment effect:
\[
\beta_1 = \pi_A - \pi_B = \theta
\]
So the regression coefficient on the treatment indicator should match the earlier difference-in-means estimate.








Two-Sample Test and Regression Comparison


Same estimate, nearly identical inference

The numbers line up almost perfectly. That is not a coincidence; it is the same comparison written in two dialects. The practical value of the regression version is that it scales gracefully. Once the problem is in regression form, adding covariates, interactions, or multiple treatments becomes straightforward.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
So far, the story has assumed a well-behaved experiment: run the test, collect the planned sample, analyze once. Real organizations are rarely that patient. A manager may want to peek at results every few hours and stop the test the moment one variant looks significant.
That instinct is understandable but statistically dangerous. A single test at the 5% significance level has a 5% chance of generating a false positive when the null is true. Repeating the test over and over on accumulating data creates multiple chances to get lucky noise. The overall false-positive rate can climb far above 5%.
To see how large that distortion can be, the simulation below creates a world where there is no true difference at all: \(\pi_A = \pi_B = 0.20\). Each experiment is checked after every 100 observations per group, up to 1,000 observations per group, and the process records whether any interim look crosses the 0.05 threshold.








Peeking Inflates Type I Error


Sequential looks create extra false-positive opportunities


Scenario
False positive rate




Single test at alpha = 0.05
5.0%


Peeking every 100 visitors per group
19.3%
















The result is sobering. A process that feels like “just checking in” can nearly quadruple the false-positive rate. For A/B testing in practice, that means patience is not merely a managerial virtue; it is part of valid inference. If early stopping is going to be allowed, it needs a method designed for sequential testing rather than ordinary repeated p-values.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Conclusion
A/B testing often looks like a lightweight product tool, but under the surface it is a compact lesson in frequentist statistics. The difference in observed conversion rates is an estimator; the Law of Large Numbers explains why it stabilizes; the bootstrap shows how uncertainty can be quantified; the Central Limit Theorem explains why Normal-based inference becomes reasonable; and hypothesis testing turns that logic into a formal decision rule.
The regression reframing shows that this same experiment fits naturally into a broader modeling framework, while the peeking example shows how easy it is to misuse statistical evidence when operational convenience takes over. That, in the end, may be the most useful lesson from the entire exercise: the strength of an A/B test comes not only from having data, but from respecting the statistical logic that makes those data trustworthy.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
text: This is one of the most assigned papers in applied microeconomics. But the real reason it still matters is not the headline result. It is the way the paper builds credibility, one comparison at a time.

title: Minimum Wages and Employment: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
In April 1992, New Jersey raised its state minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05 per hour, while neighboring Pennsylvania left its minimum wage unchanged. Card and Krueger (1994) used this policy contrast to study one of the most classical questions in labor economics: does a higher minimum wage reduce employment? Rather than relying on aggregate state-level series, they surveyed 410 fast-food restaurants in New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania before and after the reform and compared employment changes across the two states. fileciteturn6file0L1-L18
This paper is influential not only because of its result, but because of its empirical strategy. The authors did not simply compare New Jersey before versus after the policy. That comparison would confound the policy effect with any broader macroeconomic change happening in 1992. Instead, they used a difference-in-differences design: New Jersey serves as the treatment group, Pennsylvania serves as the control group, and the identifying idea is that common shocks affecting both states can be differenced out. The paper also goes one step further by comparing low-wage and high-wage stores within New Jersey, creating an internal validity check on the Pennsylvania control group. fileciteturn6file0L29-L44 fileciteturn6file0L104-L118
In this post, I replicate the key descriptive and reduced-form results from the paper. Specifically, I reproduce the full Table 2, the first three rows of Table 3, and all five columns of Table 4. I then add two visual analyses to clarify the logic of the difference-in-differences design and the role of robustness checks in the regression-adjusted models.

title: Minimum Wages and Employment: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
Research Design and Data Construction
The empirical design is unusually clean. Fast-food stores in New Jersey were exposed to the minimum wage increase, while stores in eastern Pennsylvania were not. Because the two areas are geographically close and economically linked, Pennsylvania provides a plausible counterfactual for what would have happened to employment in New Jersey in the absence of the policy. The paper also emphasizes that the increase occurred during a recession, which makes a simple before-after comparison particularly misleading and makes the control group especially important. fileciteturn6file0L45-L66
The public data file contains 410 observations, one for each store, with information from two survey waves. Employment is measured as full-time-equivalent employment, defined as the number of full-time workers plus managers plus half the number of part-time workers. I follow the authors’ SAS check program exactly when constructing employment, employment change, the wage-gap measure, and the indicators for chain and location. The gap variable is especially important in Table 4: it measures the proportional wage increase needed for a store to comply with the new New Jersey minimum wage, and equals zero for all Pennsylvania stores. fileciteturn6file0L138-L147 fileciteturn6file1L1-L13
The main outcome in the regression section is:
\[
\Delta E_i = E_{i,2} - E_{i,1}
\]
where \(E_{i,1}\) and \(E_{i,2}\) are wave-1 and wave-2 full-time-equivalent employment levels for store \(i\). The core difference-in-differences estimate compares New Jersey’s employment change to Pennsylvania’s employment change.

Replication of Table 3: The Core Difference-in-Differences Result
Table 3 contains the paper’s central descriptive comparison. The authors report mean FTE employment before the policy, mean FTE employment after the policy, and the change in mean employment across states. The key object is the difference in differences:
\[
(\overline{E}^{NJ}*{after} - \overline{E}^{NJ}*{before}) - (\overline{E}^{PA}*{after} - \overline{E}^{PA}*{before}).
\]

The pattern is the same as in the original paper. New Jersey stores began with lower average FTE employment than Pennsylvania stores, but after the minimum wage increase, employment in New Jersey rose slightly while employment in Pennsylvania fell. The raw difference in differences is therefore positive. This is already enough to reject the simplest competitive-model prediction that a binding minimum wage increase must mechanically reduce employment in the treated market.
What makes this result persuasive is not the before-after increase in New Jersey alone. If I only looked at New Jersey, I would conclude that employment rose. But that would ignore the fact that Pennsylvania employment fell at the same time. The control group tells us that there was a common negative shock in the regional economy during 1992. Once that broader decline is accounted for, New Jersey performed better than the counterfactual implied by Pennsylvania. That is the logic of the design.









This figure makes the design transparent. Pennsylvania experiences a decline in employment between the two waves, while New Jersey does not. The positive gap between the two changes is precisely the estimate that drives the paper.

title: Minimum Wages and Employment: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
section: Replication of Table 4: Why the Regression Models Matter
text:
Replication of Table 4: Why the Regression Models Matter
Table 4 is where the paper moves from descriptive difference-in-differences to regression-adjusted reduced-form models. This table matters because it addresses a natural question: is the positive employment effect sensitive to how the model is specified? If the answer were yes, the result would be fragile. If the answer is no, the causal interpretation becomes more credible.
The regression models take the form
\[
\Delta E_i = a + bX_i + cNJ_i + \varepsilon_i
\]
or
\[
\Delta E_i = a' + b'X_i + c'GAP_i + \varepsilon_i'.
\]
The first specification uses a New Jersey treatment dummy. The second replaces that dummy with a continuous measure of treatment intensity, the wage gap. This is an important conceptual step: the paper is not only asking whether treated stores differ from controls, but also whether stores that should have been more strongly affected by the law experienced different employment changes. fileciteturn6file0L119-L152

My replication closely matches the published coefficients. Column (i) reproduces the simple regression version of the New Jersey-versus-Pennsylvania difference in differences. The coefficient on the New Jersey dummy is positive, which is the regression counterpart of the positive gap from Table 3. Column (ii) then adds controls for chain and ownership. The coefficient barely changes, which is an important robustness result: the relative employment gain in New Jersey is not being driven by differences in store composition.
Columns (iii) through (v) are even more interesting. Here the paper replaces the state dummy with the initial wage gap. The estimated coefficient remains positive. This matters because the wage-gap measure varies not only across states but also within New Jersey. In other words, the paper is no longer relying solely on a treatment-versus-control comparison; it is exploiting treatment intensity. Stores that had farther to raise wages were more exposed to the policy. A positive coefficient on the wage gap therefore strengthens the substantive interpretation of the result.
Column (v) is the strongest specification in the table. Once region dummies are added, the model compares higher-gap and lower-gap stores within narrower geographic areas. This makes the test more demanding by absorbing region-specific demand shocks. The estimated gap coefficient stays positive, but the standard error increases and statistical significance weakens. I do not see this as a contradiction of the earlier results. Instead, it illustrates a core lesson in empirical work: stronger controls can reduce omitted-variable concerns, but they can also reduce usable identifying variation and magnify attenuation from measurement error. Card and Krueger explicitly discuss that tradeoff. fileciteturn6file0L153-L174









This figure makes the role of Table 4 easier to see than the table alone. The estimated coefficient stays positive across all five models. What changes is not the direction of the estimate, but the way the model attributes variation and the precision with which that variation can be measured. That is exactly why Table 4 is central to the paper: it shows that the headline result is not a one-specification accident.

title: Minimum Wages and Employment: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
section: An Internal Validity Check within New Jersey
text:
An Internal Validity Check within New Jersey
One of the smartest features of the paper is that it does not rely only on Pennsylvania as a control group. It also compares stores within New Jersey by their initial wage level. Stores already paying relatively high wages before the reform should have been less affected by the new minimum wage, while low-wage stores should have been more exposed. If the Pennsylvania control group is reasonable, then high-wage New Jersey stores should behave more like Pennsylvania stores than like low-wage New Jersey stores.









The negative value for the high-wage group should not be interpreted as an employment rate decline or as evidence against the main result. It simply means that average FTE employment at those stores fell between the two survey waves. That pattern is useful because these stores were largely unaffected by the minimum wage increase. Their decline therefore looks more like the broader regional downturn captured by Pennsylvania. In that sense, the within-New-Jersey comparison supports the logic of the control group rather than undermining it. fileciteturn6file0L105-L118

title: Minimum Wages and Employment: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
Conclusion
This replication supports the paper’s main empirical conclusion: in this setting, the 1992 increase in New Jersey’s minimum wage does not appear to have reduced employment in the fast-food sector. The descriptive difference-in-differences estimate is positive, and the regression-adjusted results remain positive across multiple specifications. That alone is already important, because it directly challenges the simplest textbook prediction that a binding minimum wage must lower employment.
What I find most compelling, however, is the structure of the identification argument. The paper does not stop at a single two-state comparison. It verifies that wages actually changed, compares employment trends across states, tests robustness with controls, and exploits treatment intensity through the wage-gap measure. It then uses within-New-Jersey comparisons as a specification check on the external control group. In other words, the contribution is not just a surprising coefficient. It is a carefully layered causal design.
That is also the biggest lesson I take from replicating this study. Good causal work is not one regression. It is a chain of evidence: descriptive balance, a credible counterfactual, transparent treatment assignment, robustness to specification changes, and internal checks that make the identifying assumptions more plausible. Card and Krueger’s paper remains a classic because it does all of those things at once.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
text:
A lot of famous empirical papers are famous because of a surprising coefficient. This one is more interesting than that. Its real strength is design. The paper takes a policy change that happened in one place but not a neighboring one, builds a believable counterfactual, and then keeps asking whether the result survives more demanding comparisons. In April 1992, New Jersey raised its state minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05 per hour, while neighboring Pennsylvania left its minimum wage unchanged. Card and Krueger used that contrast to study a classic question in labor economics: when wages are pushed up by policy, what happens to employment?
The appealing part of the setting is that the policy creates a very natural comparison. If New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania were exposed to the same regional economy but only New Jersey changed its minimum wage, then Pennsylvania becomes a useful proxy for what New Jersey might have looked like without the law. That is the intuition behind the difference-in-differences design.
The paper is also much smarter than a simple before-versus-after comparison. It does not stop with “New Jersey changed.” It asks whether the comparison group looks reasonable, whether the wage increase actually bound for treated stores, whether the result survives controls, and whether stores that were more exposed to the law behaved differently from stores that were already paying relatively high wages.


The data contain two survey waves for 410 fast-food restaurants in New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania. Employment is measured in full-time equivalents:
$$ =====================

The main object of interest is the change in employment from wave 1 to wave 2:
\[
\Delta E_i = E_{i,2} - E_{i,1}.
\]
And the core design compares that change in New Jersey to the corresponding change in Pennsylvania.



A nice feature of the paper is that it begins with descriptive facts instead of jumping straight into regression. That is exactly the right instinct. Before asking whether the minimum wage affected employment, it makes sense to check two simpler things:
Were New Jersey and Pennsylvania stores broadly comparable to begin with? Did wages in New Jersey actually move after the law?

Two things stand out immediately.
First, the two states are not identical, but they are close enough to make the comparison meaningful. Store mix is fairly similar, starting wages are nearly the same before the policy, and weekday operating hours are also very close. That does not prove parallel trends, but it makes the design much more plausible than if the two groups had looked wildly different from the start.
Second, the treatment is unmistakably real. After the law, average starting wages rise sharply in New Jersey and hardly move in Pennsylvania. The share of New Jersey stores paying exactly $5.05 also jumps dramatically. This matters because the policy should only affect employment if it actually binds for treated stores. The descriptive evidence shows that it did.

Here is the key logic.
If I only looked at New Jersey, I would say employment went up a little. But that would be too quick, because Pennsylvania was also changing over the same period. In fact, Pennsylvania employment fell. Once that broader decline is used as the baseline, New Jersey looks stronger relative to the counterfactual than a simple before-after comparison would suggest.
That is what the difference-in-differences estimate is doing. It removes the change that seems to belong to the common environment and asks what is left over.

A common mistake is to treat regression as if it automatically creates causality. That is not what is happening here. The causal leverage still comes from the design. There are two especially useful ideas in this table.
The first is that the New Jersey dummy barely changes when chain and ownership controls are added. That is reassuring. It suggests the main result is not coming from something trivial like one state having more Burger King stores or more company-owned stores.
The second is more interesting. The later columns replace the simple New Jersey indicator with a wage-gap measure — in other words, a measure of how exposed each store was to the new law. That is a more demanding test. It no longer asks only whether New Jersey differs from Pennsylvania. It asks whether stores that had farther to raise wages show systematically different employment changes.
That is a stronger idea than a plain treated-versus-untreated comparison.












One of the most elegant parts of the paper is that it does not rely only on Pennsylvania as a comparison group. It also asks whether stores within New Jersey behaved differently depending on how exposed they were to the new law.
That idea is subtle and important. If a store was already paying relatively high wages before the reform, then the new minimum wage should have mattered less there. By contrast, a low-wage store should have been much more exposed. So comparing these groups inside New Jersey creates a kind of internal validity check.









The negative value for the high-wage group does not mean an employment rate fell. It simply means average FTE employment at those stores declined between the two survey waves. And that is exactly why this comparison is interesting. High-wage stores were less exposed to the law, so if they behave more like the Pennsylvania comparison group, that supports the logic of the design rather than undermining it.



The most important lesson here is not just that the famous result is surprising. It is that the paper earns that result.
It begins by checking that the policy actually changed wages. It makes the control group legible. It shows the core difference-in-differences estimate in the simplest possible way. It then moves into more demanding models, not to create causality from scratch, but to see whether the original story still makes sense once the comparison is tightened.
That is what good causal work looks like. It is not one regression with a significant coefficient. It is a chain of reasoning in which the design comes first, the statistics support the design, and every extra model has a clear job to do.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
section: What I take away from this paper
text:
What I take away from this paper
What stays with me is less the headline and more the discipline of the design.
The paper first shows that the treatment really changed wages. Then it shows the comparison group is at least plausible. Then it gives the core difference-in-differences comparison in its simplest form. Then it asks whether that story survives when the model is tightened and when exposure is measured more directly.
That ordering matters.
The lesson is not that “regression proves causality.” The lesson is that good empirical work makes its identifying logic visible, then keeps stress-testing it. This paper is still useful because it teaches that habit extremely well.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
text:
Why this paper still matters
Card and Krueger’s paper is often remembered for one claim: a higher minimum wage did not seem to reduce fast-food employment. That is the sentence everyone quotes.
But stopping there misses the interesting part.
The paper is valuable because it shows what empirical credibility looks like when the treatment is not randomly assigned. New Jersey raised its minimum wage in April 1992, from $4.25 to $5.05. Nearby Pennsylvania did not. That policy contrast created a setting in which the authors could compare employment changes across places that were geographically close and economically linked. More importantly, they did not treat that contrast as automatically convincing. They kept asking whether the comparison held up under tighter tests.
That is what makes the paper worth revisiting. It is not just a famous result. It is a useful example of how to reason about causal identification.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
section: The setup in one clean equation
text:
The setup in one clean equation
The data contain two survey waves for fast-food restaurants in New Jersey and eastern Pennsylvania. Employment is measured in full-time equivalents, or FTEs:
\[
\text{FTE employment}
=
\text{full-time employees}
+ 0.5 \times \text{part-time employees}
+ \text{managers}.
\]
The outcome of interest is the change in employment from the first wave to the second:
\[
\Delta E_i = E_{i,2} - E_{i,1}.
\]
The core design then asks a simple question:

Did employment change more in New Jersey than in Pennsylvania after New Jersey’s minimum wage increase?

That is the entire logic of difference-in-differences in this paper.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
section: What has to be true before the design is believable
text:
What has to be true before the design is believable
Before thinking about regression, two basic checks matter.
First, the comparison group has to be reasonably credible. New Jersey and Pennsylvania stores do not need to be identical, but they should look similar enough that their short-run employment movements can be meaningfully compared.
Second, the treatment has to be real. If New Jersey restaurants were already paying wages above the new minimum, then the law would not bind and there would be little reason to expect an employment response at all.
Those checks are not secondary. They are the foundation.

Three details matter here.
The first is that the two states look close enough on several pre-policy margins to make the comparison intelligible. Store mix is not identical, but it is not wildly different either. Starting wages are nearly the same before the law. Weekday operating hours are also close. None of that proves parallel trends, but it makes the comparison more defensible.
The second is that the policy clearly bites. After the law, starting wages jump in New Jersey and barely move in Pennsylvania. The share of New Jersey stores paying exactly $5.05 also shoots up. That is crucial. A policy cannot affect behavior through wages unless it actually forces wages up.
The third is more subtle: the paper does not begin with an identification claim. It begins with legibility. That is good empirical style. Before asking whether the policy changed employment, the authors first show that it changed wages.

section: The nine numbers that make the method click
text:
The nine numbers that make the method click
Difference-in-differences sounds abstract when introduced as notation. In practice, the idea is very concrete. We only need four averages and two changes.








The nine numbers behind the design

Read the table row by row.
If we only looked at New Jersey, we would conclude that employment rose slightly. But that would be incomplete, because Pennsylvania was not flat over the same period. Employment there fell. Difference-in-differences uses that Pennsylvania decline as the baseline for the broader environment and asks whether New Jersey moved differently relative to it.
So the design is not saying, “employment increased in New Jersey.” It is saying, “employment in New Jersey changed more favorably than we would have expected given what happened in a nearby untreated comparison group.”
That is a much more careful claim.









The figure helps for one reason: it makes the counterfactual visible. The downward movement in Pennsylvania is doing real work. It is the piece that keeps the analysis from collapsing into a naive before-after comparison.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
section: Why the regression table is not the source of causality
text:
Why the regression table is not the source of causality
A lot of students learn this paper and come away thinking Table 4 is where causality appears. That is backwards.
The causal leverage is already in the design. The role of the regression table is to test whether the design-driven result looks fragile or stable once the comparison is made tougher.

There are two parts of this table that matter.
The first is the boring but reassuring one: adding chain and ownership controls barely changes the New Jersey coefficient. That tells us the basic comparison is not being driven by an obvious compositional story like one state having more company-owned stores or more restaurants from a particular chain.
The second is the more important move. The later columns stop using a simple New Jersey dummy and instead use a wage-gap measure. That variable captures how strongly each store was exposed to the law based on its initial wage. A store already paying relatively high wages should have little gap. A store starting far below $5.05 should have a large one.
That shift is conceptually valuable because it makes the test more demanding. The question is no longer just whether treated places differ from untreated places. It becomes whether more exposed stores show systematically different employment changes than less exposed stores.
That is much closer to the actual mechanism.









What I like about this figure is not that every interval is tight. They are not. What matters is that the paper keeps moving in the right direction methodologically: from a broad treatment-control contrast toward more granular exposure-based reasoning.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
section: The smartest check in the paper happens inside New Jersey
text:
The smartest check in the paper happens inside New Jersey
This is the part I think people remember less than they should.
The paper does not rely only on Pennsylvania as a comparison group. It also asks whether New Jersey stores behaved differently depending on how exposed they were to the new minimum wage.
That internal comparison is powerful because it addresses a natural worry: what if New Jersey and Pennsylvania were simply on different employment paths for reasons unrelated to the wage law?
Inside New Jersey, that interstate story cannot do the same work. Stores that were already paying high wages should have been less affected by the policy. Stores starting near $4.25 should have been much more exposed. If those groups move differently, the interpretation becomes more convincing.









This graph is easy to misread, so it helps to be precise.
The fact that the high-wage group has a negative mean change does not mean the paper has suddenly found a collapse in some employment rate. It simply means average FTE employment fell between the two survey waves for stores that were already paying relatively high starting wages.
That is exactly why the comparison is informative. Those stores were the least exposed to the new law. If they look more like the untreated Pennsylvania group, while low-wage New Jersey stores look different, the internal logic of the paper gets stronger.
In other words, the paper is not betting everything on one external control group. It is checking whether the mechanism shows up where it should.

title: What Happened When New Jersey Raised the Minimum Wage?
section: What the paper does not prove
text:
What the paper does not prove
This is also worth saying clearly.
The paper does not prove that minimum wages can never reduce employment. It does not identify a universal law. It studies one policy episode in one industry, in one region, over a short horizon.
That is not a weakness. It is what credible empirical work looks like. Good papers usually answer a narrower question than people later attribute to them.
A better reading is this: in the setting the authors study, the data do not support the simple competitive prediction that a higher minimum wage mechanically reduced fast-food employment. And the reason that conclusion is persuasive is not a single table. It is the accumulation of descriptive checks, cross-state comparisons, within-state exposure contrasts, and robustness exercises.

Experiment design, uncertainty, and decision-making under evidence


This post starts with a simple newsletter sign-up experiment, but the real focus is not the coding. It is the logic of experimentation: what random assignment buys us, why sampling uncertainty matters, and how treatment effects can be interpreted through both hypothesis testing and regression.

Causal Identification (MGTA 495)


A classic policy paper, rewritten as a readable causal story


This post revisits Card and Krueger’s study of New Jersey’s 1992 minimum wage increase. Instead of reproducing the paper section by section, I focus on the identification logic: why eastern Pennsylvania works as a comparison group, what difference-in-differences removes, and why the paper’s stronger tests matter for credibility.
